walkthrough.title: Gather the Cards
walkthrough.text: There are ten different card types in the game, each with unique sushi artwork. Give the deck a good shuffle so everything is nicely mixed up.

walkthrough.title: Deal Based on Player Count
walkthrough.text: Now let's deal cards to each player. With two players, deal ten cards each. With three players, deal nine each. With four players, deal eight each. With five players, deal seven each.

walkthrough.title: Set Aside the Draw Pile
walkthrough.text: Let's put the remaining cards face down in the center of the table. This is our draw pile for rounds two and three. We will play three rounds total, dealing fresh hands each round.

walkthrough.title: Prepare for Scoring
walkthrough.text: Let's grab some paper and a pen to keep score, or use the score tracker in the app. Each player picks up their dealt hand and keeps it secret. We are ready to start our sushi feast!

summary.title: Set Aside the Draw Pile
bullets: Leftover cards face down in center

summary.title: Prepare for Scoring
bullets: Pen and paper or app score tracker
Keep hand secret — ready to play

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Let's learn how Sushi Go! works. This is a card-drafting game where everyone plays at the same time. We will pick cards, pass our hands, and try to collect the best sushi combinations over three rounds.

walkthrough.title: Pick and Reveal
walkthrough.text: Let's start our first turn. Everyone secretly picks one card from their hand and places it face down in front of them. Once everyone is ready, we all flip our chosen cards face up at the same time.

walkthrough.title: Pass Your Hand
walkthrough.text: After revealing, pass your remaining hand to the player on your left. Pick up the new hand you receive from your right. We keep picking, revealing, and passing until all cards are played.

walkthrough.title: Set Collection Scoring
walkthrough.text: Let's learn the set cards. Tempura scores five points for a pair but zero for a single. Sashimi scores ten points for a set of three but zero for fewer. Dumplings scale up: one is one point, two is three, three is six, four is ten, and five or more is fifteen.

walkthrough.title: Nigiri and Wasabi
walkthrough.text: Nigiri cards score on their own. Egg nigiri is one point, salmon is two, and squid is three. If you play wasabi first, the next nigiri you play on top of it triples in value. Squid on wasabi is nine points, the biggest single combo in the game.

walkthrough.title: Maki Rolls
walkthrough.text: Maki roll cards show one, two, or three roll icons. At the end of each round, the player with the most total maki roll icons scores six points. The player with the second most scores three points. If there is a tie, split the points evenly and round down.

walkthrough.title: Chopsticks and Pudding
walkthrough.text: Chopsticks let you play two cards on a future turn by swapping the chopsticks back into the passing hand and saying "Sushi Go!" Pudding cards are kept until the end of the game. After round three, the most pudding scores six points and the fewest loses six points.

walkthrough.title: End of Game
walkthrough.text: After three rounds of drafting and scoring, add up all three round scores and then add the pudding bonus or penalty. The player with the highest total wins. If there is a tie, the player with the most pudding cards wins.

summary.title: Pick and Reveal
bullets: Choose 1 card secretly → place face down

summary.title: Pass Your Hand
bullets: Repeat pick → reveal → pass until hands empty

summary.title: Nigiri and Wasabi
bullets: Egg 1 pt, Salmon 2 pts, Squid 3 pts

summary.title: Maki Rolls
bullets: Count maki icons at round end

summary.title: Chopsticks and Pudding
bullets: Chopsticks: swap back to play 2 cards in one turn
Pudding: kept until game end → most +6, fewest −6

summary.title: End of Game
bullets: Highest total wins; tiebreak = most pudding

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Sushi Feast
walkthrough.text: We will walk through a practice round with four players, each starting with eight cards. Pay attention to how drafting decisions play out and what combinations to watch for.

walkthrough.title: First Pick
walkthrough.text: Everyone looks at their hand. Player one sees a squid nigiri, a wasabi, two tempura, and other cards. They take the wasabi, planning to pair it with the squid nigiri next turn. Player two takes a maki roll with three icons. Player three takes a sashimi. Player four takes a dumpling.

walkthrough.title: Pass and Pick Again
walkthrough.text: Everyone passes their hand to the left. Player one receives a new hand that contains the squid nigiri they were hoping for. They play it on their wasabi for nine points. Player two takes another maki roll. Player three takes a second sashimi. Player four takes another dumpling.

walkthrough.title: A Beginner Mistake: Chasing Sashimi
walkthrough.text: Player three now has two sashimi and needs a third to score ten points. But here is the problem. The remaining hands have no more sashimi. Player three is stuck with two sashimi worth zero points. They wasted two picks on nothing.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Know When to Abandon a Set
walkthrough.text: This is a classic beginner trap. Sashimi requires three cards for any points, so it is risky. If you do not see a third sashimi coming through the draft, switch to something else. Two wasted picks can cost you the game. Tempura is safer because it only needs two.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Round Drafting
walkthrough.text: Let's continue. Player four picks up a third dumpling, bringing their total to three for six points. Player one grabs a tempura. Player two keeps collecting maki rolls and now has five icons total. Player three pivots and picks up a chopsticks card for a future double play.

walkthrough.title: A Beginner Mistake: Ignoring Pudding
walkthrough.text: A pudding card appears in the draft. Player two passes on it because it does not score this round. Player four also ignores it. But pudding is scored at the very end of the game, and the player with the fewest puddings loses six points. That penalty is brutal.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Respect Pudding
walkthrough.text: Never completely ignore pudding. You do not need the most, but having zero or very few across three rounds can cost you the game with that minus six penalty. Pick up at least one or two puddings over the course of the game to stay safe.

walkthrough.title: Chopsticks in Action
walkthrough.text: Player three uses their chopsticks on the next turn. They pick two cards from their hand, place both face down, and swap the chopsticks card back into the passing hand while calling out "Sushi Go!" This lets them grab two valuable cards in one turn to catch up.

walkthrough.title: Scoring the Round
walkthrough.text: All cards are played. Let's score. Player one has wasabi plus squid nigiri for nine points and one tempura for zero. Player two has six maki icons for first place, earning six points. Player three recovered with chopsticks but ended with only four points. Player four has three dumplings for six points.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You saw how wasabi plus nigiri is powerful, how sashimi can be a trap, and why pudding matters. Keep these lessons in mind for a real game.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Draft
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your real game, here are three things to remember.

First, check out General Tips for drafting strategies and reading the table.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick list of all card types and scoring.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

Focus: drafting decisions and set building

bullets: Check out General Tips → drafting strategies
Reference the Appendix → card types and scoring
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Complete Your Sets
walkthrough.text: The most important rule in Sushi Go! is to finish what you start. A single tempura or one or two sashimi score zero points. Before you commit to a set, make sure you can realistically complete it within the round.

walkthrough.title: Read the Draft
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to what cards pass through your hands. If you see lots of dumplings circulating, they are available. If sashimi disappears quickly, someone else is collecting them. Use this information to decide what to pursue.

walkthrough.title: Wasabi Timing
walkthrough.text: Wasabi is best taken early when you still have many picks left to find a nigiri. Taking wasabi on your last few picks is risky because you might not see any nigiri. The dream combo is wasabi plus squid nigiri for nine points.

walkthrough.title: Hate Drafting
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the best play is to take a card not for yourself but to deny it to an opponent. If your neighbor needs one more sashimi for ten points, grab it even if it gives you zero. Denying ten points is worth more than most cards you could take for yourself.

walkthrough.title: Maki Roll Strategy
walkthrough.text: Maki rolls are reliable points. First place gets six points every round, which adds up over three rounds. If nobody else is fighting for maki, even a few rolls can guarantee you first. Watch what your neighbors are collecting.

walkthrough.title: Chopsticks Timing
walkthrough.text: Take chopsticks early in the round when you still have many turns ahead. Using chopsticks lets you grab two great cards on one turn, which is especially powerful when you spot a perfect pair late in the round.

walkthrough.title: Dumpling Power
walkthrough.text: Dumplings scale dramatically. One dumpling is just one point, but five dumplings is fifteen points. If you start collecting dumplings early and nobody else contests you, they can be the highest-scoring card type in the game.

walkthrough.title: Balance Short and Long Term
walkthrough.text: Every round matters, but so does the endgame pudding scoring. Do not sacrifice round points to hoard pudding, but do not ignore pudding entirely either. Aim for a balance where you score well each round while keeping a safe pudding count.

summary.title: Complete Your Sets
bullets: Only commit if you can finish the set

summary.title: Read the Draft
bullets: Track what cards circulate vs. disappear
Infer opponents' collections from passing patterns

summary.title: Wasabi Timing
bullets: Take wasabi early when nigiri is still available

summary.title: Hate Drafting
bullets: Blocking 10-pt sashimi > most cards for yourself

summary.title: Chopsticks Timing
bullets: Take early in round for max flexibility
Double-pick is strongest late when options narrow

summary.title: Balance Short and Long Term
bullets: Score well each round AND keep safe pudding count
Don't sacrifice round pts for pudding or vice versa

walkthrough.title: Card Counting
walkthrough.text: There are exactly fourteen tempura, fourteen sashimi, and fourteen dumplings in the deck. Knowing the total card counts helps you estimate what is still available in the draft. If you have seen twelve tempura already, there are only two left.

walkthrough.title: Positional Drafting
walkthrough.text: The player on your right feeds you cards. Watch what they collect because the cards they leave behind are what you will see. Similarly, the cards you pass go to your left neighbor. Consider what you are giving them when you make your picks.

walkthrough.title: First Pick Priority
walkthrough.text: Your first pick each round sets the tone. In general, the strongest first picks are wasabi, high-icon maki rolls, or the first dumpling when nobody else is competing for them. Sashimi is a weaker first pick because it needs two more cards to pay off.

walkthrough.title: Chopsticks Combo Plays
walkthrough.text: The best time to use chopsticks is when two high-value cards appear in the same hand. Ideal combos include grabbing a tempura pair at once, or snagging the last sashimi and a squid nigiri. Holding chopsticks too long wastes a pick.

walkthrough.title: Maki Roll Math
walkthrough.text: Maki roll scoring rewards the leader disproportionately. Six points for first versus three for second means tying for first and splitting six points gives you only three each. Sometimes it is better to let someone else win maki and focus on sets where you score alone.

walkthrough.title: Pudding Endgame Calculations
walkthrough.text: With ten pudding cards in the deck and three rounds of play, the pudding distribution usually ranges from zero to four per player. Having just two puddings is often enough to avoid the penalty. Track opponents' pudding counts to know when you are safe.

walkthrough.title: Player Count Adjustments
walkthrough.text: In a two-player game, you see more of the deck and can predict better. In five-player games, hands are smaller and more volatile. Adjust your risk tolerance for sashimi and dumplings based on how many players are competing for the same cards.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Efficiency
walkthrough.text: Every pick should aim for the highest points per card. Wasabi plus squid is nine points from two cards. Five dumplings is fifteen from five cards but only three points per card. One tempura pair is five points from two cards. Always compare the efficiency of your options before committing.

summary.title: Card Counting
bullets: Track what's been seen to estimate remaining supply

summary.title: Positional Drafting
bullets: Right neighbor feeds you → watch their picks
You feed left neighbor → consider what you pass

summary.title: Chopsticks Combo Plays
bullets: Use when 2 high-value cards appear together

summary.title: Maki Roll Math

summary.title: Player Count Adjustments
bullets: Adjust sashimi/dumpling risk by player count

entries.definition: A set-collection card. Collect two for five points. A single tempura scores zero. There are fourteen in the deck.
A set-collection card. Collect three for ten points. Fewer than three scores zero. There are fourteen in the deck.
A scaling set card. Points increase with quantity: one is one point, two is three, three is six, four is ten, five or more is fifteen. Fourteen in the deck.
Cards showing one, two, or three roll icons. At round end, most icons scores six points, second most scores three. Ties split evenly, rounded down. Twelve total cards.
A nigiri card worth one point. Can be tripled to three points when placed on wasabi. Five cards in the deck.
A nigiri card worth two points. Can be tripled to six points when placed on wasabi. Ten cards in the deck.
A nigiri card worth three points. Can be tripled to nine points when placed on wasabi, the highest single combo. Five cards in the deck.
A modifier card that triples the value of the next nigiri played on it. Worth zero on its own. Six cards in the deck.
Kept between rounds and scored at game end. Most pudding earns six points, fewest loses six points. In two-player games, no penalty for fewest. Ten cards in the deck.
On a future turn, play two cards and return chopsticks to the passing hand. Say 'Sushi Go!' when activating. Four cards in the deck.
The core mechanic of picking one card from your hand and passing the rest. All players draft simultaneously each turn.
Taking a card not for your own score but to deny it to an opponent. Useful to block set completions like sashimi or tempura.
One complete cycle of drafting until all hands are empty, followed by scoring. The game consists of three rounds.
Hands are always passed to the left after each pick. This is consistent throughout all three rounds.
If total scores are tied after three rounds, the player with the most pudding cards wins.
